Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## ON THE IMPLICIT BIASES OF ARCHITECTURE & GRADIENT DESCENT

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Do neural networks generalise because of bias in the functions returned by gradient descent, or bias already present in the network architecture?

## _¿Por qué no los dos?_

This paper finds that while typical networks that fit the training data already generalise fairly well, gradient descent can further improve generalisation by selecting networks with a large margin. This conclusion is based on a careful study of the behaviour of infinite width networks trained by Bayesian inference and finite width networks trained by gradient descent. To measure the implicit bias of architecture, new technical tools are developed to both _analytically bound_ and _consistently estimate_ the average test error of the neural network–Gaussian process (NNGP) posterior. This error is found to be already better than chance, corroborating the findings of Valle-Pérez et al. (2019) and underscoring the importance of architecture. Going beyond this result, this paper finds that test performance can be substantially improved by selecting a function with much larger margin than is typical under the NNGP posterior. This highlights a curious fact: _minimum a posteriori_ functions can generalise best, and gradient descent can select for those functions. In summary, new technical tools suggest a nuanced portrait of generalisation involving both the implicit biases of architecture and gradient descent.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Following an influential paper by Zhang et al. (2017), the basic question of _why neural networks generalise_ is generally regarded to be open. The authors demonstrate a surprising fact: deep learning generalises even when the neural network is expressive enough to represent functions that do not generalise. In turn, this implies that the theory of Vapnik & Chervonenkis (1971)—based on uniform convergence of train error to population error—does not explain generalisation in neural networks.

Several hypotheses have since been proposed to fill this theoretical vacuum. One prominent hypothesis states that, while most neural network solutions do not generalise well, there is an implicit bias in the kinds of functions returned by gradient descent (Soudry et al., 2018). In sharp contrast, a second hypothesis states that the solution space of a neural network is dominated by simple functions, while the complex kinds of functions that overfit are relatively rare (Valle-Pérez et al., 2019).

This latter hypothesis dovetails with a particular “PAC-Bayesian” theorem of McAllester (1998), which bounds the average population error of all classifiers consistent with a training sample. If this bound is small, then the complex functions that overfit must indeed be rare. In more technical terms, the PAC-Bayesian theorem can provide a meaningful certificate of generalisation even for machine learning models with an infinite Vapnik-Chervonenkis dimension, or an arbitrarily large number of parameters, by properly accounting for the measure of those very complex functions.

This paper takes a more nuanced position between these two hypotheses. While the _average_ population error of all neural networks that fit a training sample is found to be good (and _certifiably good_ by the PAC-Bayesian theorem), it is still possible for certain networks with special properties to perform substantially _better than average_ (and likewise substantially better than the PAC-Bayes bound). Moreover, gradient descent may be used to specifically target these special networks. This subtly counters a position put forward by Mingard et al. (2021), which suggests that gradient descent may be well-modelled as sampling randomly from a particular Bayesian posterior distribution.

To support these claims, a careful study of the behaviour of both infinite width and finite width neural networks is conducted. In particular, this paper makes the following technical contributions:

- Section 3 _Implicit Bias of Architecture_ . A purely analytical PAC-Bayes bound (Theorem 2) on the population error of the neural network–Gaussian process (NNGP) posterior for binary classification is derived. The bound furnishes an interpretable measure of model complexity that depends on both the architecture and the training data. This exact analytical bound improves upon an approximate computational approach due to Valle-Pérez et al. (2019).

- Section 4 _Testing the Bound_ . The new bound is found to be both non-vacuous and correlated with the test error of finite width multilayer perceptrons trained by gradient descent. This provides supporting evidence for the important role of architecture in generalisation, as put forward by Valle-Pérez et al. (2019). Still, a gap exists with the performance of gradient descent.

- Section 5 _Implicit Bias of Gradient Descent_ . Going further beyond the work of Valle-Pérez et al. (2019), a new theoretical tool (Theorem 3) is developed to enable consistent estimation of the average error of the NNGP posterior on a given holdout set. The average is found to be significantly worse than the holdout performance of Gaussian process draws with large margin. The experiment is repeated for finite width neural networks trained by gradient descent, and the same qualitative phenomenon persists. This finding demonstrates the ability of gradient descent to select large margin functions with vanishing posterior probability that nonetheless generalise significantly better than the posterior average.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Margin-based generalisation theory** A rich body of work explores the connection between margin and generalisation. For instance, Bartlett et al. (2017) propose a margin-based complexity measure for neural networks derived via Rademacher complexity analysis, and Neyshabur et al. (2018) derive a similar result via PAC-Bayes analysis. Neyshabur et al. (2019) test these bounds experimentally, finding them to be vacuous and to scale poorly with network width. Biggs & Guedj (2021) further develop this style of bound. Margin-based PAC-Bayes bounds go back at least to the work of Herbrich (2001), who derived such a bound for linear classification. The standard idea is that a solution with large margin implies the existence of nearby solutions with the same training error (but perhaps smaller margin), facilitating a more spread out PAC-Bayes posterior. This style of margin-based PAC-Bayes bound does not provide guidance on whether the original large margin classifier should generalise better or worse than the additional nearby classifiers included in the posterior.

**Non-vacuous bounds for neural networks** Aside from PAC-Bayes, many styles of generalisation bound for neural networks are vacuous (Dziugaite & Roy, 2017). Even many PAC-Bayes bounds are vacuous (Achille & Soatto, 2018; Foret et al., 2021) due to their choice of PAC-Bayes prior. Dziugaite & Roy (2017) construct a _non-vacuous_ weight space PAC-Bayes bound by iteratively optimising both the PAC-Bayes prior and posterior by gradient descent. Wu et al. (2020) extend this approach to involve Hessians. Meanwhile, Valle-Pérez et al. (2019) instantiate a non-vacuous PAC-Bayes bound in the function space of neural networks via the NNGP correspondence (Neal, 1994; Lee et al., 2018). The evaluation of this bound involves an iterative and statistically inconsistent expectation-propagation approximation. Unlike this paper’s purely analytical bound in Theorem 2, these non-vacuous bounds are all evaluated by iterative computational procedures.

**Implicit bias of gradient descent** Much research has gone into the role that gradient descent plays in neural network generalisation. For instance, Zhang et al. (2017) suggest that gradient descent may converge to solutions with special generalisation properties, while Wilson et al. (2017) suggest that gradient descent may have a more favourable implicit bias than the Adam optimiser. Azizan et al. (2021) make a related study in the context of mirror descent. Keskar et al. (2017) point to an implicit bias present in mini-batch stochastic gradient descent, but Geiping et al. (2021) argue against the importance of stochasticity. Meanwhile, Soudry et al. (2018) observe how gradient descent combined with certain loss functions converges to max-margin solutions; Wei et al. (2018) make a similar observation. On a different tack, Mingard et al. (2021), Valle-Pérez & Louis (2020) and Valle-Pérez et al. (2019) argue that the implicit bias of neural architecture is more important than that of gradient descent for understanding generalisation, and so long as gradient descent does not select solutions pathologically, generalisation should occur.

## 3 IMPLICIT BIAS OF ARCHITECTURE

This section derives an analytical bound on the population error of an infinitely wide neural network averaged over all weight configurations that attain 0% train error—in other words, the average population error of the NNGP posterior. Since this quantity assesses the performance of all solutions rather than just those returned by gradient descent, it is intended to measure the implicit bias of architecture. In Section 4, the bound is found to be non-vacuous for multilayer perceptrons. The source of a substantial gap with the results of gradient descent training is investigated in Section 5, and ultimately attributed to an additional implicit bias of gradient descent: namely _margin_ .

A classifier _f_ shall depend on a weight vector _w ∈_ Ω, where Ω denotes the _weight space_ . In particular, for an input _x_ the prediction is given by sign _f_ ( _x_ ; _w_ ).

One is interested in the relationship between the _train error εS_ of a classifier on a dataset _S_ to the _population error εD_ on a data distribution _D_ . For a weight vector _w ∈_ Ω, these are defined as:
The _solution space VS_ := _{w ∈_ Ω _| εS_ ( _w_ ) = 0 _}_ denotes those classifiers that attain zero train error.

## 3.2 GEOMETRY OF SOLUTIONS IN FUNCTION SPACE

The function space _FS_ is defined to be the set of outputs that the classifier _f_ can realise on dataset _S_ :
In weight space the geometry of solutions can be arbitrarily complicated, but in function space the solutions occupy the single orthant picked out by the binary training labels.

To measure the volume of the solution space, one can define a measure P on weight space. It is convenient to enforce P[Ω] = 1 so that P is a probability measure. In the absence of a better alternative, P is usually set to a multivariate Gaussian or uniform distribution. The measure P on weight space induces a measure P on function space via the relation:
The volume of solutions is denoted P[ _VS_ ], and can be computed either in weight or function space. In function space, P[ _VS_ ] is an orthant probability. The situation is visualised in Figure 1.

## 3.3 PAC-BAYES THEORY

PAC-Bayes relates the volume of solutions to their average population error. The following result was derived by Valle-Pérez et al. (2019) as a corollary of a theorem due to Langford & Seeger (2001). The result is similar in form to a theorem of McAllester (1998).

**Theorem 1** (A PAC-Bayesian theorem) **.** _First, fix a probability measure_ P _over the weight space_ Ω _of a classifier. Let S denote a training set of n datapoints sampled iid from the data distribution D and let VS denote the corresponding solution space. Consider the population error_ 0 _≤ εD_ ( _w_ ) _≤_ 1 _of weight setting w ∈_ Ω _, and its average over the solution space εD_ ( _VS_ ) := E _w∼_ P[ _εD_ ( _w_ ) _| w ∈ VS_ ] _. Then, for a proportion_ 1 _− δ of draws of the training set S,_

For large _n_ , the ln 2 _n/δ_ term is negligible and the result says that the population error averaged over solutions is less than the ratio of the negative log volume of solutions to the number of training points.

Let S denote a training set of n datapoints sampled iid from the data distribution D, let y ∈{±_ 1 _}[n] denote the binary vector of training labels, and let VS denote the corresponding solution space. Consider the population error_ 0 _≤ εD_ ( _w_ ) _≤_ 1 _of weight setting w ∈_ Ω _, and its average over the solution space εD_ ( _VS_ ) := E _w∼_ P[ _εD_ ( _w_ ) _| w ∈ VS_ ] _. _where the complexity measures C_ 0 _and C_ 1 _are defined in Lemma 1._

Since the complexity measure _C_ 1 is an analytical function of the NNGP covariance matrix Σ and the binary vector of training labels _y_ , Theorem 2 is an analytical generalisation bound for the NNGP posterior. For large _n_ , the result simplifies to: _εD_ ( _VS_ ) ⪅ _C_ 0(Σ _, y_ ) _/n ≤_ 1 _/_ 5 + _C_ 1(Σ _, y_ ) _/n_ . So the bound depends on the ratio of complexity measures _C_ 0 and _C_ 1 to the number of datapoints _n_ .

To gain further intuition about Theorem 2, consider two special cases. First, suppose that the neural architecture induces no correlation between any pair of distinct data points, such that Σ = I. Then _εD_ ( _VS_ ) ⪅ _C_ 0(I _, y_ ) _/n_ = ln 2 _≈_ 0 _._ 7 and the bound is worse than chance. This corresponds to _pure memorisation_ of the training labels. Next, suppose that the neural architecture induces strong intra-class correlations and strong inter-class anti-correlations, such that Σ _ij_ = _yiyj_ . Although this Σ is singular, it may be seen directly that P[ _VS_ ] = 1 _/_ 2. Then by Theorem 1, _εD_ ( _VS_ ) ⪅ ln 2 _/n_ which is much better than chance for large _n_ . This corresponds to _pure generalisation_ from the training labels. Interpolating between these two extremes would suggest that a good neural architecture would impose a prior on functions with strong intra-class and weak inter-class correlations.

## 4 TESTING THE BOUND

This section compares the generalisation bound for infinite width networks (Theorem 2) to the performance of finite width multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) trained by gradient descent. The bound is found to be non-vacuous and correlated with the effects of varying depth and dataset complexity. Still, there is a substantial gap between the bound and gradient descent, which is investigated in Section 5.

Three modified versions of the MNIST handwritten digit dataset (LeCun et al., 1998) of varying “hardness” were used in the experiments, as detailed in Figure 2 (top left). MLPs were trained with _L_ layers and _W_ hidden units per layer. Specifically, each 28px _×_ 28px input image _x_ was flattened to lie in R[784] , and normalised to satisfy _∥x∥_ 2 = _√_ 784. The networks consisted of an input layer in R[784] _[×][W]_ , ( _L −_ 2) layers in R _[W][ ×][W]_ , and an output layer in R _[W][ ×]_[1] _._ The nonlinearity _ϕ_ was set to _ϕ_ ( _z_ ) := _√_ 2 _·_ max(0 _, z_ ). For this architecture, the width _W →∞_ kernel is the _compositional arccosine kernel_ described in Theorem 4 in Appendix A.3. For the finite width networks, the training loss was set to square loss using the binary training labels as regression targets, and the networks were trained for 100 epochs using the Nero optimiser (Liu et al., 2021) with an initial learning rate of 0.01 decayed by a factor of 0.9 every epoch, and a mini-batch size of min(50 _,_ training set size) data points. The final train error was 0% in all reported experiments. All bounds were computed with a failure probability of _δ_ = 0 _._ 01, and _C_ 0 was estimated using 10[6] Monte-Carlo samples. All experiments were run on one NVIDIA Titan RTX GPU.

The generalisation bound of Theorem 2 was first compared across three datasets of varying complexity. The network architecture was set to a depth _L_ = 7 MLP, and the bound was computed via MonteCarlo estimation of _C_ 0. The results are shown in Figure 2 (top right). The bound was found to reflect the relative hardness of the datasets. For _random labels_ , the bound was vacuous as desired. Next, the generalisation bound was compared against the empirical performance of a depth _L_ = 7 MLP trained on the _decimal digits_ dataset. The results are shown in Figure 2 (bottom left). While loose compared to the holdout error of the finite width network, the bound is still non-vacuous. Finally, the effect of varying network depth was investigated on the _decimal digits_ dataset. Two depths were compared: _L_ = 2 and _L_ = 7. The results are shown in Figure 2 (bottom right). The bounds (computed via Monte-Carlo estimation of _C_ 0) appear to predict the relative holdout performance of the two architectures at finite width. These results corroborate those of Valle-Pérez & Louis (2020) but without the use of the statistically inconsistent expectation-propagation approximation.

## 5 IMPLICIT BIAS OF GRADIENT DESCENT

In Section 3, a bound was derived on the average population error of all infinitely wide neural networks that fit a certain training set. Since the bounded quantity measures the performance of all solutions rather than just those returned by gradient descent, it is intended to assess the implicit bias of architecture. In Section 4, this bound was tested and found to be non-vacuous. Still a substantial gap was found between the bound and the performance of finite width networks trained by gradient descent. This gap could arise for several potential reasons:

- i) slackness in the bounding technique;

- ii) a difference between infinite width and finite width neural networks;

- iii) an additional implicit bias of gradient descent.

This section tests these various possibilities, ultimately concluding that gradient descent does have an important additional implicit bias: the ability to control the margin of the returned network.

## 5.1 AN EXACT FORMULA FOR THE AVERAGE HOLDOUT ERROR OF SOLUTIONS

To investigate whether slackness in the bounding technique is responsible for the gap, an additional theoretical tool was developed: a formula for the holdout error of a binary classifier averaged over the solution space. In contrast to Theorem 2 which gives an upper bound on population error, Theorem 3 is an equality and therefore does not suffer from slackness. The proof is given in Appendix A.1.

**Theorem 3** (Average holdout error of a binary classifier) **.** _First, fix a probability measure_ P _over the weight space_ Ω _of a binary classifier. Let S denote a training set and let VS denote the solution space. For a holdout set T of m datapoints, consider the holdout error_ 0 _≤ εT_ ( _w_ ) _≤_ 1 _of weight setting w ∈_ Ω _, and its average over the solution space εT_ ( _VS_ ) := E _w∼_ P[ _εT_ ( _w_ ) _| w ∈ VS_ ] _. Then:_

In words, the average holdout error over solutions equals the reduction in the volume of solutions when the training set is augmented with a negated holdout point, averaged over the holdout set. For an infinitely wide neural network, Equation 11 involves computing a sum of ratios of Gaussian orthant probabilities. These probabilities can be consistently estimated by Equation 8 in Lemma 1.

## 5.2 THREE-WAY COMPARISON OF HOLDOUT ERROR

Armed with Theorem 3, this subsection makes a three-way comparison between the holdout error of infinite width networks averaged over solutions, the holdout error of finite width networks averaged over solutions, and the holdout error of finite width networks trained by gradient descent.

To compute the holdout error of finite width networks averaged over solutions, weight vectors were randomly sampled and all non-solutions were discarded. To make this process computationally tractable, a very small training set was used consisting of only 5 samples from _binary digits_ . A holdout set of 50 datapoints was used, and the network was set to a 7-layer MLP. The results were:

average holdout error at infinite width: 0 _._ 337 _±_ 0 _._ 001; average holdout error at width 10,000: 0 _._ 33 _±_ 0 _._ 01; gradient descent holdout error at width 10,000: 0 _._ 178 _±_ 0 _._ 007 _._

Based on these results, three comments are in order. First, the close agreement between the average holdout error at finite and infinite width suggests that the infinite width limit may not be responsible for the significant gap observed in Section 4. Second, the significant gap between the gradient descent holdout error and the holdout error averaged over solutions suggests slackness in the bounding technique in Theorem 2 may also not be the main culprit. Third, the significant gap between the gradient descent holdout error and the holdout error averaged over solutions suggests that gradient descent _does_ have an extra implicit bias. The next subsection attempts to diagnose this implicit bias.

## 5.3 DIAGNOSIS: MARGIN

This subsection finds that gradient descent has an important implicit bias in determining the margin of the returned network. This conclusion is made by studying the generalisation error of functions along the _label ray_ in function space—depicted in Figure 3 (top left). For a training set _S_ with a vector of binary labels _y ∈{±_ 1 _}[n]_ , a function _α-far_ along the label ray refers to the point _αy ∈FS_ .
For the case of a zero mean NNGP, the predictive distribution on a holdout set _T_ conditioned on training labels _α_ -far along the label ray is given by Bishop (2006, Chapter 2.3.1):

where Σ _T S_ is the covariance between holdout and train inputs, and Σ _ST_ , Σ _SS_ and Σ _T T_ are defined analogously. For large _α_ , predictions are driven by the posterior mean, whereas for small _α_ , they are driven by random fluctuations. So one expects that letting _α →∞_ should improve holdout error.
For the case of gradient descent training, functions _α_ -far along the label ray can be returned by minimising the following loss function:

A subtle but important point is that for gradient descent training with Nero (Liu et al., 2021) the norms of the weight matrices are constrained, thus _α_ controls a properly normalised notion of margin.

An experiment was performed to measure the holdout error of networks _α_ -far along the label ray—for both NNGP draws and neural networks selected by Nero—for _α_ ranging from 10 _[−]_[2] to 10[2] . As can be seen in Figure 3 (top right), varying _α_ appears to directly control the holdout error, despite all solutions attaining 0% train error. Moreover, large _α_ solutions significantly outperform the average holdout error over the solution space. This suggests that gradient descent possesses a significant implicit bias in its ability to control margin—going beyond the implicit bias of architecture.

To sanity check this result, experiments from Section 4 were repeated with the loss function _Lα_ =0 _._ 1 replacing _Lα_ =1. As can be seen in Figure 3 (bottom), the holdout performance was significantly diminshed at _α_ = 0 _._ 1, as was the gap with the PAC-Bayes bound from Theorem 2.

## 6 DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSION

This paper has explored the separate implicit biases of architecture and gradient descent. Section 3 derived an analytical generalisation bound on the NNGP posterior, Section 4 found this bound to be non-vacuous, while Section 5 showed that large margin functions substantially outperform the bound.

The findings in this paper support the importance of the _implicit bias of architecture_ : reproducing the findings in Valle-Pérez & Louis (2020) but with improved technical tools, the average generalisation performance of architecture was already found to be good. But the _implicit bias of gradient descent_ was also found to be important. In particular, in contrast to an assumption made by Valle-Pérez & Louis (2020) that gradient descent “samples the zero-error region close to uniformly”, it was found that gradient descent can be used to target _zero-error functions with large margin_ . This also subtly counters a proposal by Mingard et al. (2021) that gradient descent acts like a “Bayesian sampler”. In particular, gradient descent can target functions with margin _α →∞_ for which the Bayesian posterior probability _→_ 0. And indeed, these _minimum a posteriori_ functions seem to generalise best.

One direction of future work suggested by these results is an improvement to the function space PAC-Bayes theory to account for margin. While margin-based PAC-Bayes bounds do already exist (Herbrich, 2001; Langford & Shawe-Taylor, 2003; Neyshabur et al., 2018), these bounds operate in weight space and further do not seem to imply an advantage to selecting the max-margin classifier over the other classifiers included in the PAC-Bayes posterior.

Ultimately, a generalisation theory that properly accounts for the various implicit biases of deep learning could provide a more principled basis for both neural architecture design as well as the design of new regularisation schemes. It is hoped that the new analytical results as well as experimental insights included in this paper contribute a step towards reaching that goal.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
**High-Level Review Feedback**

This paper tackles a timely and important question in deep learning theory: whether neural network generalization stems primarily from architectural biases or from the implicit bias of gradient descent. The author stakes out a nuanced middle position between the Valle-Pérez et al. (2019) and Mingard et al. (2021) "architecture-centric" view and the gradient-descent-centric view, arguing that both matter—architecture provides a generalization baseline, while gradient descent further improves performance by selecting large-margin solutions. This is a worthwhile contribution, and the claimed technical tools (an analytical PAC-Bayes bound on the NNGP posterior and a consistent estimator for its average holdout error) appear potentially valuable to the community. The writing is generally clear and the introduction effectively situates the contributions, though the somewhat flippant "¿Por qué no los dos?" framing and the deferred presentation of theorems make it hard to evaluate the core technical claims from the introduction alone. The most significant concerns at this stage are: (1) the central empirical claim—that gradient descent selects atypically high-margin functions that generalize better than the posterior average—directly contradicts Mingard et al. (2021), so the strength and scope of the supporting evidence (especially whether it holds beyond MLPs and simple settings) will be critical; (2) it is unclear how large the claimed gap between posterior-average performance and gradient-descent performance actually is, and whether it is robust across architectures, datasets, and training regimes; and (3) the paper should more carefully distinguish its margin-based findings from the extensive prior literature on margin and generalization (Bartlett et al., Neyshabur et al., Biggs & Guedj), which is acknowledged but not yet clearly differentiated in terms of conceptual novelty. Overall, the paper has the ingredients of a solid contribution, but its ultimate value will depend heavily on the tightness of the analytical bound, the rigor of the consistency results, and the breadth of empirical support for the gradient-descent-selects-margin claim.

comments.quote: Then _εD_ ( _VS_ ) ⪅ _C_ 0(I _, y_ ) _/n_ = ln 2 _≈_ 0 _._ 7 and the bound is worse than chance.
comments.explanation: I checked this special case. If Σ = I, the network outputs on the n training points are independent standard Gaussians, so P[V_S] = 1/2^n, and −ln P[V_S] = n ln 2. By Theorem 1, the bound ε_D(V_S) ⪅ (−ln P[V_S])/n = ln 2 ≈ 0.7. That matches the final numerical claim. However, the text writes this as C_0(I, y)/n = ln 2, which would require C_0(I, y) = n ln 2. Comparing with the other special case given immediately after — 'Σ_ij = y_i y_j ... P[V_S] = 1/2, Then by Theorem 1, ε_D(V_S) ⪅ ln 2/n' — there the authors correctly write ln 2 / n (since −ln(1/2) = ln 2). So the quantity being compared across the two cases is inconsistent: in the first case they effectively wrote the bound as (n ln 2)/n = ln 2, and in the second case they wrote it as ln 2 / n. The final numerical value 0.7 for the first case is correct, but equating C_0(I,y)/n with ln 2 is either a notation slip (C_0 should be n ln 2 here, not ln 2) or the division by n is erroneous. Either way the expression as written is inconsistent with the parallel treatment of the second case.

comments.title: Conclusion about slackness reversed in logical reasoning
comments.quote: Second, the significant gap between the gradient descent holdout error and the holdout error averaged over solutions suggests slackness in the bounding technique in Theorem 2 may also not be the main culprit.
comments.explanation: I initially parsed this carefully to check the logic. The authors compare (a) gradient descent holdout error ≈ 0.178, versus (b) the *actual* average holdout error over solutions ≈ 0.337 (computed via Theorem 3, which is exact/no slackness). A gap between these two quantities tells us about gradient descent's bias, not about slackness in Theorem 2. To assess slackness in Theorem 2, one would need to compare the Theorem 2 *bound* to the Theorem 3 exact average. If the Theorem 3 exact average (≈0.337) is close to the Theorem 2 bound, then the bound is tight (not slack); if far, the bound is slack. The quoted sentence seems to conflate these. The actual reason slackness is ruled out would come from comparing the Theorem 2 bound to the Theorem 3 exact value, not from the GD vs. average gap. As written, the justification for ruling out slackness (reason i) uses the same evidence as for establishing GD's implicit bias (reason iii), which is logically incorrect — that evidence only supports (iii). The statement may be salvageable if one assumes the Theorem 2 bound is close to 0.337 (which would indeed show low slackness), but this comparison is not made explicit in the passage.

comments.title: Claim about MAP functions conflates margin with posterior probability
comments.quote: In particular, gradient descent can target functions with margin _α →∞_ for which the Bayesian posterior probability _→_ 0. And indeed, these _minimum a posteriori_ functions seem to generalise best.
comments.explanation: Initially I read this as a provocative framing of the main finding. But on closer inspection there's a real conceptual issue: the phrase 'minimum a posteriori' (contrasted with MAP = maximum a posteriori) is being used to describe functions at α → ∞ whose posterior density vanishes. However, under a Gaussian process posterior, the posterior density of a function αy also goes to 0 as α → ∞ simply because it's in the tail of a Gaussian — this doesn't mean it's a 'minimum' in any meaningful sense (the posterior density over function outputs on S has its maximum at a finite α, not at α=0). The authors seem to be equating 'posterior probability → 0' with 'minimum a posteriori,' but α = 0 (the origin) also has vanishing posterior mass for a continuous GP. The rhetorical claim that these are 'minimum a posteriori' functions that generalize best is imprecise and could mislead readers about the Bayesian interpretation. The substantive finding (large-margin functions generalize better than the posterior average) is valid, but the 'minimum a posteriori' framing overstates/misframes it.

comments.title: Normalization claim for Nero and α as 'properly normalised margin' under-justified
comments.quote: A subtle but important point is that for gradient descent training with Nero (Liu et al., 2021) the norms of the weight matrices are constrained, thus _α_ controls a properly normalised notion of margin.
comments.explanation: The authors are making a key claim that justifies interpreting α as margin rather than just an output scale: because Nero constrains weight matrix norms, scaling the target by α actually forces the network to produce larger margin (rather than just rescaling outputs via weight growth). This is central to the Section 5 conclusion that GD's implicit bias is about margin control. However, the argument is given in a single sentence with no quantitative statement of what 'constrained' means (Nero constrains per-neuron norm, not spectral norm of the full matrix, and the relation to output margin is non-trivial), and no empirical check that ||f(x;w)||/||w|| or some analogous normalized margin actually scales with α. A reader trying to reproduce or verify the argument would need substantially more detail. Given this is the linchpin of the paper's interpretation of GD's implicit bias, the justification is thin.

comments.quote: Depending on the degree of _anisotropy[√] n_ det ΣΣ _[−]_[1] _−_ I inherent in the covariance matrix Σ, Equation 8 captures how the orthant probability may either be exponentially amplified or suppressed compared to 1 _/_ 2 _[n]_ .
comments.explanation: The prose describes an 'anisotropy' quantity of the form sqrt(det Σ) · (Σ^{-1} - I) or similar, which is not straightforwardly the standard form of a Gaussian orthant expression. comments.title: Claim that finite vs infinite width agreement rules out width effect is only partial
comments.quote: First, the close agreement between the average holdout error at finite and infinite width suggests that the infinite width limit may not be responsible for the significant gap observed in Section 4.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper tackles an important and timely question—how much neural network generalisation should be attributed to architectural priors versus the implicit bias of gradient descent—and the framing is compelling, with a clear attempt to reconcile two prominent but often opposed viewpoints. The introduction is generally well written, technically confident, and effective in positioning the work relative to PAC-Bayes, NNGP, margin-based explanations, and prior empirical claims; the stated contributions are also concrete and potentially significant, especially the promise of analytical PAC-Bayes bounds and consistent estimation of NNGP posterior error. That said, the beginning somewhat overstates the conceptual resolution before the technical evidence has been presented, and several claims would benefit from sharper qualification: for example, what exactly counts as “typical networks,” how “architecture bias” is isolated from training dynamics, and in what sense gradient descent “selects” minimum-a-posteriori or large-margin functions should be made more precise early on. The informal tone in the abstract, especially “¿Por qué no los dos?”, is memorable but may be risky for a top-tier conference paper unless the rest of the exposition is exceptionally rigorous. The most significant issue is that the paper appears to rely heavily on comparisons between NNGP posterior averages, large-margin posterior samples, and finite-width gradient-descent solutions; the validity of the conclusions will depend critically on whether these objects are truly comparable and whether the experimental protocol rules out confounders such as finite-width effects, kernel mismatch, data preprocessing, or hyperparameter tuning. Overall, the paper appears promising, original, and well motivated, but its contribution will stand or fall on the rigor and transparency of the theoretical assumptions and empirical methodology supporting the claimed separation between architectural bias and optimization bias.

comments.title: Gradient-descent claims are stronger than the optimizer evidence shown
comments.quote: finite width networks trained by gradient descent
comments.explanation: The paper frames several conclusions as being about gradient descent, but the experimental methods describe finite-width networks trained with the Nero optimiser, minibatches, and a learning-rate schedule rather than plain gradient descent. Because the paper is specifically concerned with optimizer implicit bias, and because different optimizers can have different implicit biases, this distinction matters. If Nero is intended only as a representative gradient-based method, that should be stated explicitly; otherwise the conclusions should be phrased as evidence about Nero or supported by controls using vanilla GD/SGD.

comments.title: “Minimum a posteriori” and posterior-probability claims need a measurable event or density interpretation
comments.quote: In particular, gradient descent can target functions with margin α →∞ for which the Bayesian posterior probability → 0. And indeed, these minimum a posteriori functions seem to generalise best.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast with Bayesian averaging is interesting: large-margin functions may lie in very low posterior-mass regions while generalising better than typical posterior draws. However, in a continuous GP/function-space posterior, individual functions or exact points such as f_S = αy have probability zero. What can tend to zero is the posterior mass of a specified event, such as {min_i y_i f(x_i) ≥ α}, or perhaps a density relative to a chosen reference measure. The phrase “minimum a posteriori functions” is also nonstandard and risks confusion with MAP, while “generalise best” sounds stronger than the evidence supports. The claim should specify the relevant measurable set or density and avoid implying that individual continuous functions have well-defined posterior probabilities.

comments.title: Better-than-chance NNGP posterior performance is stated too broadly
comments.quote: This error is found to be already better than chance, corroborating the findings of Valle-Pérez et al. (2019) and underscoring the importance of architecture.
comments.explanation: The statement is supported for structured MNIST variants, where the architecture-induced NNGP posterior has nontrivial generalisation before considering gradient descent. But the experiments also include a random-label setting where no input-label relationship exists and the bound is deliberately vacuous. As written, the statement could be read as a general claim across all studied settings. A qualifier such as “on structured datasets” or “in the non-random-label settings” would avoid the overstatement.

comments.title: “All classifiers” obscures dependence on the chosen prior measure
comments.quote: which bounds the average population error of all classifiers consistent with a training sample.
comments.explanation: The relevant PAC-Bayes quantity is not an unweighted or uniform average over all consistent classifiers, which is generally not meaningful for an infinite hypothesis class. It is an average under a specified prior/posterior measure, here essentially the architecture-induced prior conditioned on consistency. Since dependence on this prior is central to the paper’s thesis, the statement would be clearer as “the prior-weighted average population error” or “the average under the architecture-induced posterior over consistent classifiers.”

comments.title: Lemma appears to require a positive-definite covariance, but is stated for an arbitrary covariance matrix
comments.quote: For a covariance matrix Σ ∈ R[n][×][n], and a binary vector y ∈{±1}[n], let p denote the corresponding Gaussian orthant probability:
comments.explanation: The later formulas and intuition appear to use quantities such as det Σ and Σ^{-1}. These are well-defined for symmetric positive-definite covariance matrices, but a covariance matrix in general may be only positive semidefinite. NNGP Gram matrices can be singular with duplicate inputs, degenerate kernels, or perfectly correlated outputs, and the paper itself later discusses a singular case Σ_ij = y_i y_j. If the lemma and resulting quantities C_0 and C_1 require invertibility, the statement should assume Σ ≻ 0 or specify a limiting, pseudoinverse, or pseudodeterminant convention.

comments.title: Function-space solution and label-ray descriptions ignore possible support/realizability restrictions
comments.quote: In weight space the geometry of solutions can be arbitrarily complicated, but in function space the solutions occupy the single orthant picked out by the binary training labels.
comments.explanation: For the vector of outputs on the training set, zero classification error is indeed the orthant condition y_i f(x_i) > 0. But if function space FS is defined as the set of outputs realizable by the architecture, the solution set is more precisely FS intersected with that orthant, not necessarily the full orthant. The same issue appears later when points αy are described as lying along the label ray in FS: for finite-width networks or singular kernels, αy need not be exactly realizable. The orthant and label-ray language is harmless for a full-support nonsingular GP, but the prose should acknowledge the support/realizability condition.

comments.title: Monte Carlo estimates are described as PAC-Bayes bounds without certified estimator uncertainty
comments.quote: All bounds were computed with a failure probability of δ = 0.01, and C_0 was estimated using 10[6] Monte-Carlo samples.
comments.explanation: A PAC-Bayes guarantee with failure probability δ applies to the exact bound quantity, or to a certified one-sided numerical estimate whose own error probability is included in the confidence accounting. A plug-in Monte Carlo estimate of an orthant probability, especially after taking logarithms, does not automatically preserve the bound. Unless the paper supplies a concentration correction or confidence interval for the Monte Carlo estimate, the resulting curves should be described as estimates of the bound rather than rigorous bounds with δ = 0.01.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of whether the depth-comparison bounds use Monte Carlo C_0 or exact C_1
comments.quote: The bounds (computed via Monte-Carlo estimation of C_0) appear to predict the relative holdout performance of the two architectures at finite width.
comments.explanation: The text says the bottom-right depth comparison uses Monte Carlo estimation of C_0, while the Figure 2 caption says the same panel uses exact computation of C_1. These are materially different procedures: C_0 is estimated by Monte Carlo, whereas C_1 is presented as an analytical bound. Because the experiment is used to argue that the analytical bound predicts the effect of depth, the paper should make clear which quantity was actually plotted.

comments.title: Finite-width prior, initialization, and solution-sampling details are underspecified
comments.quote: For the finite width networks, the training loss was set to square loss using the binary training labels as regression targets, and the networks were trained for 100 epochs using the Nero optimiser...
comments.explanation: The finite-width experiments omit important details about the prior/initialization distribution: variance and fan-in scaling, output-layer treatment, presence or absence of biases, and whether the finite-width prior matches the NNGP limit being used for comparison. The later finite-width solution-average experiment is also underspecified: it does not state the weight measure used for rejection sampling, the number of accepted solutions, the acceptance rate, or convergence checks. These details are consequential because both trained solutions and prior-conditioned solution averages can depend strongly on initialization and scaling.

comments.title: The label-aligned covariance example could be read as using a data-dependent prior
comments.quote: Next, suppose that the neural architecture induces strong intra-class correlations and strong inter-class anti-correlations, such that Σ_ij = y_i y_j.
comments.explanation: The calculation is correct if the covariance aligns with the label vector: the Gaussian lies on the label direction and the orthant probability is 1/2. But PAC-Bayes requires the prior, including the NNGP covariance induced by the architecture, to be fixed before observing the sample labels. Because the covariance is written directly in terms of y, readers could interpret the example as choosing the prior after seeing labels. The text should clarify that this is an idealized architecture whose input-induced covariance happens to align with the true labeling rule, not a data-dependent prior.

comments.title: Modified MNIST datasets are not specified enough to reproduce the comparisons
comments.quote: Three modified versions of the MNIST handwritten digit dataset (LeCun et al., 1998) of varying “hardness” were used in the experiments, as detailed in Figure 2 (top left).
comments.explanation: The figure gives the high-level task definitions, but reproducibility requires additional details: whether training subsets are class-balanced, how train/holdout splits are sampled, how random labels are assigned, how parity is mapped to ±1, and whether nested subsets are reused across training-set sizes. These choices can affect both empirical holdout curves and label-dependent PAC-Bayes quantities.

comments.title: Exact holdout formula does not by itself eliminate population-bound slack
comments.quote: In contrast to Theorem 2 which gives an upper bound on population error, Theorem 3 is an equality and therefore does not suffer from slackness.
comments.explanation: Theorem 3 may exactly characterize the empirical holdout error averaged over the zero-training-error set, which is useful. But this equality is for a fixed finite holdout set, not the population error bounded in Theorem 2, and the orthant ratios may still be estimated numerically. Thus it removes one source of analytical slack for an empirical holdout quantity, but it does not by itself show that slackness in the population bound is unimportant.

comments.title: Very small-task solution-average comparison only weakly supports conclusions about the larger-experiment gap
comments.quote: To make this process computationally tractable, a very small training set was used consisting of only 5 samples from binary digits. A holdout set of 50 datapoints was used, and the network was set to a 7-layer MLP.
comments.explanation: The tiny experiment is a useful sanity check because rejection sampling from the prior conditional on zero training error is otherwise infeasible. However, the Section 4 gap was observed for larger training sets, different MNIST variants, and bounds rather than exact holdout averages. Agreement between finite- and infinite-width solution averages on a 5-sample binary-digits task does not strongly rule out finite-width effects or bound looseness as contributors to the larger-experiment gap.

comments.title: Statistical meaning of the reported error bars is unclear
comments.quote: average holdout error at infinite width: 0.337 ± 0.001; average holdout error at width 10,000: 0.33 ± 0.01; gradient descent holdout error at width 10,000: 0.178 ± 0.007.
comments.explanation: The passage does not say whether the ± values are standard deviations, standard errors, confidence intervals, or variability over training sets, holdout sets, accepted prior samples, Monte Carlo orthant estimates, or optimizer initializations. This matters because the holdout set has only 50 datapoints and the infinite-width estimate involves estimated orthant ratios. Without clarifying the uncertainty source, claims of a significant gap are harder to interpret statistically.

comments.title: Large-α conditioning improves holdout error only if the posterior mean has the correct sign
comments.quote: For large α, predictions are driven by the posterior mean, whereas for small α, they are driven by random fluctuations. So one expects that letting α →∞ should improve holdout error.
comments.explanation: Under GP conditioning, the conditional mean on holdout points scales with α while the conditional covariance is independent of α, so large α makes predictions approach the sign of the kernel-interpolating posterior mean. But that limit improves holdout error only where y_T^{(i)}(Σ_TSΣ_SS^{-1}y)_i is positive; it worsens error where this quantity is negative and remains chance-level where it is zero. The empirical result may hold for the studied MNIST variants, but the prose should not present improvement as a general consequence of increasing α without this correctness condition.

comments.title: The α-margin experiment does not by itself establish an implicit gradient-descent bias
comments.quote: This suggests that gradient descent possesses a significant implicit bias in its ability to control margin—going beyond the implicit bias of architecture.
comments.explanation: The experiment shows that high-margin solutions can be targeted and that such solutions can generalise better than typical NNGP posterior solutions. However, the margin is deliberately controlled by changing the regression target scale α, which is an explicit intervention in the objective. In addition, for finite-width networks, minimizing the α-target loss only guarantees outputs near αy if regression error is actually driven close to zero, not merely if classification error is zero. The argument also relies on Nero constraints making α a normalized margin, but the paper does not spell out exactly which norms are constrained or how the normalized margin is defined. The conclusion would be stronger if the authors reported attained margins/training losses, specified the Nero normalization, and separated “accessibility under a target-scaling intervention” from “implicit bias under a fixed learning problem.”

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper tackles an important and timely question—how much neural network generalisation should be attributed to architectural priors versus the implicit bias of gradient descent—and the framing is compelling, with a clear attempt to reconcile two prominent but often opposed viewpoints. The introduction is generally well written, technically confident, and effective in positioning the work relative to PAC-Bayes, NNGP, margin-based explanations, and prior empirical claims; the stated contributions are also concrete and potentially significant, especially the promise of analytical PAC-Bayes bounds and consistent estimation of NNGP posterior error. That said, the beginning somewhat overstates the conceptual resolution before the technical evidence has been presented, and several claims would benefit from sharper qualification: for example, what exactly counts as “typical networks,” how “architecture bias” is isolated from training dynamics, and in what sense gradient descent “selects” minimum-a-posteriori or large-margin functions should be made more precise early on. The informal tone in the abstract, especially “¿Por qué no los dos?”, is memorable but may be risky for a top-tier conference paper unless the rest of the exposition is exceptionally rigorous. The most significant issue is that the paper appears to rely heavily on comparisons between NNGP posterior averages, large-margin posterior samples, and finite-width gradient-descent solutions; the validity of the conclusions will depend critically on whether these objects are truly comparable and whether the experimental protocol rules out confounders such as finite-width effects, kernel mismatch, data preprocessing, or hyperparameter tuning. Overall, the paper appears promising, original, and well motivated, but its contribution will stand or fall on the rigor and transparency of the theoretical assumptions and empirical methodology supporting the claimed separation between architectural bias and optimization bias.

comments.title: Claims about gradient descent are stronger than the optimizer evidence shown in the methods
comments.quote: finite width networks trained by gradient descent
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a standard shorthand for gradient-based training, and in many papers “gradient descent” is used loosely to include stochastic or variant optimizers. However, checking the later experimental description in the provided context, the finite-width networks are trained “using the Nero optimiser” with minibatches and a learning-rate schedule, not plain gradient descent. This matters here because the paper’s central framing is specifically about the implicit bias of gradient descent, and the related-work section itself notes that different optimizers can have different implicit biases. If Nero is merely intended as an implementation of a broad gradient-based procedure, that should be stated; otherwise, the conclusions should be phrased as evidence about the particular optimizer used, or supported by controls using vanilla GD/SGD. As written, the passage risks attributing to gradient descent an effect that may depend on Nero’s normalization or update rule.

comments.title: “Minimum a posteriori” is not well-defined for the continuous NNGP posterior
comments.quote: This highlights a curious fact: _minimum a posteriori_ functions can generalise best, and gradient descent can select for those functions.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast with Bayesian averaging: Section 5 apparently finds that large-margin functions lie in very low posterior-probability regions yet perform better than the posterior average. That is an interesting point. But the phrase “minimum a posteriori functions” is technically problematic. In a continuous GP/function-space posterior, individual functions typically have posterior probability zero, so ranking individual functions by posterior probability is not canonical; one would need to speak about densities relative to a chosen reference measure, small posterior-mass sets, or events such as exceeding a margin threshold. Moreover, “generalise best” sounds like an optimality claim over all functions or all posterior regions, whereas the described evidence supports the weaker claim that selected large-margin functions can generalise substantially better than the posterior average. The rhetorical inversion of MAP is memorable, but without qualification it may mislead readers about what is mathematically defined and what has been shown.

comments.title: Better-than-chance NNGP posterior performance is stated too broadly
comments.quote: This error is found to be already better than chance, corroborating the findings of Valle-Pérez et al. (2019) and underscoring the importance of architecture.
comments.explanation: The intended point is clear and is supported later for structured MNIST variants: the architecture-induced NNGP posterior already has nontrivial generalisation before considering gradient descent. However, the full context also includes a random-label condition where no input-label relationship exists and the bound is deliberately vacuous. Thus the statement is not true as an unqualified claim about the NNGP posterior error across the studied settings. A careful reader can infer that the authors mean natural/structured tasks, but the abstract wording could be read as a general empirical conclusion. Adding a qualifier such as “on structured datasets” or “in the non-random-label settings” would avoid the overstatement.

comments.title: “All classifiers” obscures the dependence on the chosen prior measure
comments.quote: which bounds the average population error of all classifiers consistent with a training sample.
comments.explanation: The surrounding paragraph correctly gestures at measure-theoretic accounting for rare complex functions, and Section 3 later defines the average over the solution space with respect to a fixed prior/architecture-induced measure. Still, this sentence by itself could be read as referring to an unweighted or uniform average over all consistent classifiers, which is not generally meaningful for an infinite hypothesis class and is not what PAC-Bayes provides. PAC-Bayes bounds an average under a specified posterior, here essentially the prior conditioned on consistency, and the result can change substantially with the choice of prior/architecture. Since that dependence is central to the paper’s thesis, it would be more precise to say “the prior-weighted average population error” or “the average under the architecture-induced posterior over consistent classifiers.”

comments.title: Lemma appears to require a positive-definite covariance, but is stated for an arbitrary covariance matrix
comments.quote: For a covariance matrix_ Σ _∈_ R _[n][×][n] , and a binary vector y ∈{±_ 1 _}[n] , let p denote the corresponding Gaussian orthant probability:
comments.explanation: The intended result is clear: the authors want to express and bound the Gaussian orthant probability induced by the NNGP covariance on the training inputs. However, the surrounding text indicates that the lemma’s formulas involve quantities such as det Σ and Σ^{-1} — for example, later intuition refers to anisotropy involving “det ΣΣ _[−]_[1] _−_ I”. That is fine when Σ is symmetric positive definite, but a covariance matrix in general is only positive semidefinite. NNGP Gram matrices can be singular, for example with duplicate inputs, degenerate kernels, or limiting cases with perfectly correlated outputs; the paper itself later discusses the singular case Σ_ij = y_i y_j. If the lemma and the resulting complexity measures C_0 and C_1 require invertibility, the statement should explicitly assume Σ ≻ 0, or explain a limiting/pseudoinverse/pseudodeterminant convention. Otherwise Theorem 2 may appear to apply to covariance matrices for which the displayed analytical expressions are not defined.

comments.title: Solution set in function space is described as an orthant, but strictly it is the realizable support intersected with an orthant
comments.quote: In weight space the geometry of solutions can be arbitrarily complicated, but in function space the solutions occupy the single orthant picked out by the binary training labels.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended geometric simplification: once one looks only at the vector of outputs on the training set, zero classification error is exactly the sign constraint y_i f(x^{(i)}) > 0, i.e. an orthant condition in the ambient R^n. This is the right intuition for the later Gaussian orthant probability calculation. However, given the preceding definition of function space FS as the set of outputs the classifier can realise on S, the solution set is more precisely FS intersected with that orthant, not necessarily the whole orthant. For a full-support nonsingular Gaussian NNGP on the training outputs this distinction is harmless, but for finite architectures with restricted image, or singular Gaussian measures, the support can be a proper subset of R^n. The probability can still be called an orthant probability in the ambient space, but the prose and Figure 1 caption could mislead readers into thinking the realizability/support constraint disappears entirely in function space.

comments.title: Monte Carlo estimates are repeatedly described as PAC-Bayes bounds without accounting for estimator uncertainty
comments.quote: All bounds were computed with a failure probability of _δ_ = 0 _._ 01, and _C_ 0 was estimated using 10[6] Monte-Carlo samples.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intent: Lemma 1 provides a Monte Carlo route to evaluating the Gaussian orthant probability, and with 10^6 samples the numerical error may be small in practice. I also noticed that the figure caption later distinguishes a “W = ∞ estimator” from a “W = ∞ bound,” which partially acknowledges the distinction. However, the prose here still says “All bounds” while describing a random Monte Carlo estimate of C0. A PAC-Bayes guarantee with failure probability δ is only valid for the exact quantity, or for a certified one-sided numerical estimate with its own error probability folded into the confidence level. An unbiased estimator of an orthant probability, or a plug-in estimate of a log-complexity, does not automatically preserve the bound because the logarithm and one-sided nature of the guarantee matter. Unless the paper provides a concentration correction or confidence interval elsewhere, the curves computed from estimated C0 should be described as estimates of the bound, not as rigorous bounds with failure probability δ = 0.01.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of whether the depth-comparison bounds use Monte Carlo C0 or exact C1
comments.quote: The bounds (computed via Monte-Carlo estimation of _C_ 0) appear to predict the relative holdout performance of the two architectures at finite width.
comments.explanation: This initially looked like a straightforward statement that the bottom-right depth comparison used the same Monte Carlo C0 procedure as the earlier dataset-complexity comparison. But the Figure 2 caption says, for the same bottom-right panel: “The bounds are computed by exact computation of _C_ 1.” These are materially different procedures: C0 is presented as estimated by Monte Carlo, while C1 is presented as the exact analytical bound. The distinction matters because the paper uses this experiment to argue that the analytical bound predicts the effect of depth. A reader cannot tell from the current text which quantity was actually plotted for the depth comparison.

comments.title: Finite-width training setup omits weight initialization and scaling details needed for reproduction and for comparison to the NNGP
comments.quote: For the finite width networks, the training loss was set to square loss using the binary training labels as regression targets, and the networks were trained for 100 epochs using the Nero optimiser (Liu et al., 2021) with an initial learning rate of 0.01 decayed by a factor of 0.9 every epoch, and a mini-batch size of min(50 _,_ training set size) data points.
comments.explanation: The optimization hyperparameters are described in useful detail, and the infinite-width kernel is referenced via the compositional arccosine kernel. What remains underspecified is the finite-width initialization: the distribution, variance/scaling by fan-in, treatment of the output layer, and any biases or absence of biases. These choices are not cosmetic here. They determine the finite-width network's initial function distribution, its relation to the stated NNGP prior, and can affect training dynamics under square loss and Nero. Since a central empirical comparison is between finite-width gradient descent and the infinite-width NNGP architecture bias, omitting the initialization/scaling details makes the experiment difficult to reproduce and weakens the precision of that comparison.

comments.title: The label-aligned covariance example could be read as using a data-dependent prior
comments.quote: Next, suppose that the neural architecture induces strong intra-class correlations and strong inter-class anti-correlations, such that Σ _ij_ = _yiyj_ . Although this Σ is singular, it may be seen directly that P[ _VS_ ] = 1 _/_ 2. Then by Theorem 1, _εD_ ( _VS_ ) ⪅ ln 2 _/n_ which is much better than chance for large _n_ .
comments.explanation: The calculation itself is right: if z is distributed along the label vector y, then the event y_i z_i > 0 for all i has probability 1/2. The concern is about the interpretation. The PAC-Bayes theorem requires the prior measure, hence the NNGP covariance induced by the architecture, to be fixed before the sample is drawn. Since Σ is written directly in terms of the observed labels y, a reader could interpret this as choosing the covariance after seeing S, which would invalidate the appeal to Theorem 1. I think the authors intend this only as an idealized architecture whose input-induced covariance happens to align with the true labeling rule. That would be fine, but the text should make that caveat explicit before invoking the PAC-Bayes theorem.

comments.title: Modified MNIST datasets are not specified enough to reproduce the reported comparisons
comments.quote: Three modified versions of the MNIST handwritten digit dataset (LeCun et al., 1998) of varying “hardness” were used in the experiments, as detailed in Figure 2 (top left).
comments.explanation: The table in Figure 2 gives the high-level idea of the three tasks—random labels, decimal digit parity, and binary digit parity—and that is enough to understand the qualitative motivation. For reproducibility, however, several consequential choices remain implicit: whether training subsets are class-balanced, how the train/holdout splits are sampled, whether the random-label task assigns independent random labels to both training and holdout examples or fixes a random relabeling, how parity is mapped to ±1, and whether the same nested subsets are used across training-set sizes. These details can affect both the empirical holdout curves and the covariance/label-dependent PAC-Bayes quantities. This is localized to the experimental section, but it would be hard to reproduce the exact plots from the description given.

comments.title: Exact holdout formula does not by itself eliminate population-bound slack
comments.quote: In contrast to Theorem 2 which gives an upper bound on population error, Theorem 3 is an equality and therefore does not suffer from slackness.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: Theorem 2 upper-bounds the NNGP posterior's population error via an orthant-probability lower bound, whereas Theorem 3 apparently gives an exact identity for the empirical holdout error averaged over the zero-training-error set. That is a useful diagnostic, and it can help separate posterior-average performance from looseness of the PAC-Bayes/orthant bound. What remains slightly problematic is the phrasing. The equality is for a fixed finite holdout set, not for the population error appearing in Theorem 2, and in practice the Gaussian orthant ratios are still estimated numerically. Thus it removes one source of analytical slack for an empirical holdout quantity, but it does not by itself show that slackness in the population bound is unimportant. A careful reader could read this as a stronger conclusion than the theorem supports.

comments.title: Finite-width solution-average experiment is underspecified
comments.quote: To compute the holdout error of finite width networks averaged over solutions, weight vectors were randomly sampled and all non-solutions were discarded.
comments.explanation: The rejection-sampling idea is conceptually correct for estimating \(\mathbb{E}_{w\sim P}[\epsilon_T(w)\mid w\in V_S]\): sample from the prior measure and condition on zero training error. However, the passage does not specify the actual finite-width measure \(P\) used for these random weights. This matters because the conditional solution average can depend strongly on the weight/bias distribution, variance scaling, initialization convention, and whether it exactly matches the finite-width prior whose infinite-width limit gives the NNGP kernel. The text also does not state how many accepted solutions were used, the acceptance rate, or how convergence of the rejection estimate was checked. Since this finite-width solution average is central to the three-way comparison, these omitted details make the procedure hard to reproduce and make it difficult to assess whether the finite- and infinite-width averages are being compared under the same prior.

comments.title: Very small-task comparison only weakly supports conclusions about the Section 4 gap
comments.quote: To make this process computationally tractable, a very small training set was used consisting of only 5 samples from _binary digits_ . A holdout set of 50 datapoints was used, and the network was set to a 7-layer MLP.
comments.explanation: I see why the authors use a tiny dataset: rejection sampling from the prior conditional on zero training error quickly becomes infeasible as \(n\) grows. The resulting experiment is a useful sanity check, especially because the finite-width and infinite-width solution averages are close. But the gap in Section 4 was observed for substantially larger training sets, different MNIST variants such as decimal digits, and bounds rather than exact holdout averages. Agreement between width 10,000 and infinite width on a 5-sample binary-digits task does not strongly rule out finite-width effects or bound looseness as contributors to the Section 4 gap in the larger experiments. The later text mostly uses cautious language such as “suggests,” but the setup risks overextending a very small-task diagnostic to a broader conclusion.

comments.title: Statistical meaning of the reported error bars is unclear
comments.quote: average holdout error at infinite width: 0 _._ 337 _±_ 0 _._ 001; average holdout error at width 10,000: 0 _._ 33 _±_ 0 _._ 01; gradient descent holdout error at width 10,000: 0 _._ 178 _±_ 0 _._ 007 _._
comments.explanation: The numerical comparison is important and the reported separation between the solution average and gradient descent is large. However, the passage does not say what the \(\pm\) values represent: standard deviation over random training sets, holdout sets, accepted prior samples, Monte Carlo orthant estimates, gradient-descent initializations, or standard error of the mean. This is especially relevant because the holdout set has only 50 datapoints, so finite-holdout variability can be non-negligible, and the infinite-width estimate involves ratios of estimated orthant probabilities. Without clarifying the source of these uncertainties, the subsequent claims of a “significant gap” are harder to interpret statistically.

comments.title: The label-ray definition assumes realizability in function space
comments.quote: For a training set _S_ with a vector of binary labels _y ∈{±_ 1 _}[n]_ , a function _α-far_ along the label ray refers to the point _αy ∈FS_ .
comments.explanation: The intended construction is clear: move in output-vector space in the direction of the label vector, so that \(y_i f(x^{(i)})=\alpha\) for all training examples and the functional margin is controlled. For a nondegenerate GP prior on the finite training set, conditioning on the observation \(f_S=\alpha y\) is a standard Gaussian-conditioning operation, so the idea is mathematically natural. The wording is still a bit too strong because \(F_S\) was defined earlier as the set of output vectors actually realizable by the network architecture. For finite-width networks, or for singular kernels/correlated datasets, it is not automatic that every point \(\alpha y\) lies in \(F_S\) exactly. The experiments train networks toward that target, but that is not the same as proving \(\alpha y\in F_S\). Rephrasing this as a direction or target in output space, with conditions under which it is realizable, would avoid a possible notation mismatch.

comments.title: Large-α conditioning improves holdout error only under an additional correctness condition
comments.quote: For large _α_ , predictions are driven by the posterior mean, whereas for small _α_ , they are driven by random fluctuations. So one expects that letting _α →∞_ should improve holdout error.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended calculation: under the GP conditioning formula, the conditional mean on the holdout set scales like αΣ_TSΣ_SS^{-1}y, while the conditional covariance is independent of α. Thus, as α grows, the sign of a draw is increasingly determined by the kernel-interpolating posterior mean rather than by posterior noise. That part is right. What does not follow in general is that holdout error should improve as α→∞. For a holdout point i, the error probability tends to 0 if y_T^{(i)}(Σ_TSΣ_SS^{-1}y)_i>0, to 1 if this quantity is negative, and to 1/2 if it is zero. Thus the limiting classifier is simply the sign of the GP posterior mean/kernel interpolant, and large α helps only on examples where that classifier has the correct sign. The empirical result may show this is true for the MNIST variants studied, but the prose currently sounds like a general consequence of increasing α. A short qualifier such as ‘provided the posterior mean has the correct sign on holdout points’ would make the statement mathematically accurate.

comments.title: Varying α via the loss target is an explicit intervention, not by itself evidence of an implicit gradient-descent bias
comments.quote: This suggests that gradient descent possesses a significant implicit bias in its ability to control margin—going beyond the implicit bias of architecture.
comments.explanation: The experiment is interesting and does support the claim that large-margin functions exist and can be found by the training procedure. However, I initially hesitated over the interpretation as an ‘implicit bias of gradient descent’, because the margin is being deliberately controlled by changing the target scale α in the loss function. That is an explicit choice of objective/hyperparameter, especially with Nero’s norm constraints making output scale correspond more directly to normalised margin. What the experiment seems to establish most directly is that gradient-based optimisation can be used to select high-margin solutions, and that these solutions generalise better than typical NNGP posterior solutions. It does not, without an additional argument about the default loss/training dynamics, show that gradient descent implicitly selects such solutions under a fixed learning problem. The conclusion would be stronger if the authors separated ‘accessibility by gradient descent under a modified target scale’ from ‘implicit bias of gradient descent’ or justified why the target-scaling intervention should count as exposing an implicit rather than explicit bias.

comments.title: Finite-width networks are called α-far along the label ray without enough verification of exact margin scale
comments.quote: For the case of gradient descent training, functions _α_ -far along the label ray can be returned by minimising the following loss function:
comments.explanation: The intended construction is clear: train a finite network with regression targets αy so that its training outputs lie near the point αy on the label ray. But for finite-width networks, minimising such a loss only guarantees being exactly α-far along the label ray if the optimisation reaches essentially zero regression error, not merely zero classification error. The later sentence says all solutions attain 0% train error, but zero train error is weaker than f_S=αy and does not determine the margin scale. Since the main claim depends on α directly controlling a normalised margin, a careful reader would want to see the attained training losses or attained signed margins y_i f(x_i) for each α, especially for small α where optimisation tolerances and sign conventions near zero could matter. This is fixable by reporting that the networks reached the intended targets to high precision, or by redefining the finite-network experiment in terms of measured margins rather than nominal α.

comments.title: Nero norm constraints are invoked too tersely to justify α as a normalised margin
comments.quote: A subtle but important point is that for gradient descent training with Nero (Liu et al., 2021) the norms of the weight matrices are constrained, thus _α_ controls a properly normalised notion of margin.
comments.explanation: The point being made is important: in positively homogeneous networks, raw output scale is not a meaningful margin unless weight norms or some equivalent scale are controlled. Nero may indeed impose normalisation constraints that make the target scale α interpretable. However, the statement is too compressed for the role it plays in the argument. Nero’s constraints are not described here in enough detail to know exactly which norms are fixed, whether this applies to all layers including the output layer, whether biases are present and constrained, and whether the relevant margin is normalised by product of layer norms, Frobenius norms, neuron-wise norms, or another architecture-aware quantity. Standard margin theory is sensitive to precisely this normalisation. The authors are likely right that Nero removes a scale degeneracy, but the paper should spell out the exact constrained quantity and the resulting definition of ‘properly normalised margin’ used in the experiment.

comments.title: ‘Posterior probability → 0’ and ‘minimum a posteriori functions’ need a precise event or density interpretation
comments.quote: In particular, gradient descent can target functions with margin _α →∞_ for which the Bayesian posterior probability _→_ 0. And indeed, these _minimum a posteriori_ functions seem to generalise best.
comments.explanation: I see the intended contrast: typical draws from the NNGP posterior conditioned on zero classification error have finite margins, while the event of increasingly large training margin should have vanishing posterior mass. That is a meaningful and interesting point. But as written, the probability statement is ambiguous in continuous function space. Exact functions, and exact points on the label ray f_S=αy, have probability zero for every α under a GP; what can tend to zero is the posterior mass of a margin event such as {min_i y_i f(x_i)≥α}, or perhaps the prior/posterior density around αy. Similarly, ‘minimum a posteriori’ is a nonstandard phrase that risks being confused with MAP, which conventionally means maximum a posteriori, and it is unclear whether the authors mean low posterior density, low posterior mass in a neighbourhood, or a limiting tail event. The claim would be clearer and technically safer if it specified the relevant measurable set or density and avoided implying that individual continuous functions have well-defined posterior probabilities.
